content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 19, 2010

Susan Strachan
Butte County Water Commission
40 Via Morro Ct
Chico, CA 95929

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-098

Dear Ms. Strachan:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as Government Code Section 1090 and common law conflict of interest, or incompatible activities restrictions your agency may impose.

QUESTION

May you make, participate in making, or influence a government decision by the Butte County Water Commission (the "BCWC") regarding its recommendation to the Butte County Board of Supervisors related to the governance structure of a proposed Integrated Regional Water Management Group (the "IRWMG") despite providing grant management services under contract to the Thermalito Water and Sewer Agency (the "TWSA")?

________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Because the Commission is not a finder of fact, the determination of whether a decision
regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on your economic interests must be left up to your informed judgment. You may
take part in a governmental decision regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG only if
all of the following conditions are met:

• The TWSA is not, and does not become, directly involved in the decision.

• There is no "nexus," as described below, between your duties owed to the TWSA and
your duties with the BCWC.

• The reasonably foreseeable financial effects on your economic interests in the TWSA and
your grant management services businesses are less than the applicable materiality
standards under Regulation 18705.3(b)(2), and the reasonably foreseeable effect on your
personal finances is less than $250 (Regulation 18705.5(a)).

• No additional facts indicate a potential financial effect on any other economic interest
you may have.

FACTS

You are a member of the BCWC, a position appointed by the Butte County Board of
Supervisors. The primary duties of the BCWC include approving and denying permits for
groundwater substitution for surface water transfers and making recommendations to the Butte
County Board of Supervisors regarding a wide range of water issues, such as the submittal of
grant proposals and general plan development.

In addition to your position with the BCWC, you are employed by the California State
University of Chico Research Foundation and also provide grant management services as a
consultant for the TWSA and several private "cleantech" companies. In a telephone
conversation on June 21, 2010, you explained that the TWSA is the only governmental agency
client of your consulting business, although you are also negotiating a contract with the Town of
Paradise. In addition, you stated that you technically provide grant management services as a
sole proprietor, but only as a subcontractor for another sole proprietor. However, you also stated
that you have jointly marketed grant management services with the other proprietor and have
previously presented your services as a partnership with the other proprietor.

In the near future, the BCWC will be making a recommendation to the Butte County
Board of Supervisors regarding the governance structure of a proposed IRWMG. IRWMGs are
collaborative entities that are formed to apply for grant funding through the Department of Water
Resources. Under state statute, at least one of the entities must have water management
authority. Currently, Butte County has entered into a memorandum of understanding with the

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 3

counties of Shasta, Tehama, Glenn, Colusa, and Sutter to form an IRWMG and to seek a state
grant. It is likely that an existing IRWMG consisting of water districts within the area will
disband should the county-based IRWMG obtain funding to develop its water management plan.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in any given governmental decision.

Step One: Are you a "public official?"

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency . . . ." (Section 82048.) As a member of the
BCWC, you are a public official within the meaning of the Act.

Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the
official endeavors or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) As a member of the BCWC, you are making, participating in
making, or influencing a governmental decision when taking part in the BCWC's
recommendations to the Butte County Board of Supervisors regarding the governance structure
of the proposed IRWMG.

Step Three: What are your "economic interests?"

Of the economic interests recognized under the Act², those interests implicated by your
account of the facts are the following:

² Our analysis is limited to the economic interests you have identified.

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 4

Business Entity – A public official has an economic interest in a business entity³ in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d); Regulation 18703.1(a) and (b).)

Source of Income – A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

Personal Financial Effects – A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Grant Management Services – You have provided the fact that you operate a grant management business as a sole proprietor. Presumably, you have an investment interest in this business entity of $2,000 or more and, therefore, an economic interest in this business entity under Section 87103(a). Regardless of Section 87103(a), as a sole proprietor, you are an employee/manager of the business entity and have an economic interest in this business entity under Section 87103(b). Moreover, if you receive income of $500 or more for your services in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision, you also have an economic interest in this business as a source of income. (Section 87103(c).)

Additionally, you have stated that you provide grant management services as a subcontractor for another sole proprietor. However, you have indicated that you have jointly marketed your grant management services with those services of the other proprietor and have previously presented your services as a partnership with the other proprietor. Under Regulation 18703.1(c), an official has an economic interest in a business entity that is "otherwise related" to another business entity in which the official has an economic interest pursuant to Section 87103(a) or (d). Similarly, under Regulation 18703.3, an official also has an economic interest in a business entity as a source of income if the business entity is "otherwise related" to another business entity in which the official has an economic interest pursuant to Section 87103(c). Business entities are "otherwise related" if any one of the following tests is met:

"(A) One business entity has a controlling ownership interest in the other business entity.

"(B) There is shared management and control between the entities. In determining whether there is shared management and control, consideration should be given to the following factors:

³ Section 82005 provides: "Business entity" means any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation, or association. This definition does not include nonprofits or governmental entities.

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 5

"(i) The same person or substantially the same person owns and manages
the two entities;

"(ii) There are common or commingled funds or assets;

"(iii) The business entities share the use of the same offices or employees,
or otherwise share activities, resources or personnel on a regular basis;

"(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working relationship between
the entities; or

"(C) A controlling owner (50% or greater interest as a shareholder or as a
general partner) in one entity also is a controlling owner in the other entity."
(Regulation 18703.1(d)(2).)

Under the facts you have presented, it appears that your grant management services
business is otherwise related to the grant management services business of the other proprietor.
Thus, it appears that you also have economic interests in the related grant management services
business as both a business entity and a source of income.

Clients of the Grant Management Services Businesses - As a sole proprietor of a grant
management services business, you may also have economic interests in the clients or customers
of this business as sources of income. Under Section 82030(a), if a public official owns a 10-
percent or greater interest in a business, customers who are sources of income to that business
are also sources of income to the public official. Provided that your income derived from any
particular client or customer aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the
governmental decision, you also have an economic interest in the client or customer as a source
of income. (Section 87103(c).)

In regards to the clients of the grant management services business for which you are
employed as a subcontractor, the Commission typically advises that a contractor is the sole
source of income to subcontractors, even though subcontractors are normally paid from funds
collected from the contractor's client. (See Sauer Advice Letter, No. A-95-373.) Moreover, a
source of income to a business entity is not typically considered a source of income to a public
official unless the official owns a 10-percent or greater interest in the business. However, we
have previously advised that a source of income to a general contractor may be considered a
source of income to a subcontractor if the general contractor's business is sufficiently related to
the subcontractor's business under the tests of Regulations 18703.3(a)(2) and 18703.1(d).
(Daysog Advice Letter, No. I-06-098.) Accordingly, you also have an economic interest in a
client or customer of the otherwise related grant management services business if your share of
the income derived from the particular client or customer aggregates to $500 or more in the 12
months prior to the governmental decision.

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 6


For purposes of this analysis, the only client of either grant management services
business you have identified is the TWSA.¹ Assuming that you have received income from the
TWSA of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision for your grant management
services, it appears that you have an economic interest in this client as a source of income
notwithstanding the fact that you provide services as a subcontractor for another sole proprietor
and do not have a direct contract with the TWSA.²

California State University of Chico Research Foundation – Assuming you have received
income of $500 or more from the foundation in the 12 months prior to the governmental
decision, you also have an economic interest in the foundation as a source of income. (Section
87102(c).) However, based upon the facts provided, there is no indication that the foundation
may be financially affected by governmental decisions regarding the governance structure of the
IRWMG. Barring additional facts, it does not appear that your economic interest in the
foundation is implicated. Thus, we do not discuss your economic interest in the foundation
further. If additional facts indicate a potential financial effect on the foundation, we recommend
seeking additional assistance providing all relevant facts.

Personal Finances – A public official always has an economic interest in his or her
personal finances. A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the
decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or
her immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?

Business Entities/Sources of Income - Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity
or source of income is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency when that
business entity or source of income, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or

¹ Based upon the facts provided, we are unable to analyze potential conflicts of interest you may have
based upon economic interests in clients, other than the TWSA, including but not limited to the private "cleantech"
companies for which you currently provide services and the Town of Paradise. Prior to making, participating in
making, or influencing any governmental decision you will need to determine whether a conflict of interest may
arise based upon economic interests in each of your clients. If you have additional questions related to any
economic interests in your clients, other than the TWSA, it is advisable that you request further assistance providing
all relevant facts.

² While the Act states that "government salary" is not included in the definition of "income" (Section
82030(b)(2)), compensation for your services as a consultant and independent contractor does not constitute
government salary. (See Rainey Advice Letter, A-98-101.)

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 7

revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."

Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in governmental
decisions are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704.1(b), 18705.1(a)(2), and
Regulation 18705.3(b).)

Grant Management Services Businesses - From the facts provided, neither your grant
management services business nor the related grant management services business initiated the
proceedings or are a party in, or subject of, the proceedings within the meaning of Regulation
18704.1. Accordingly, your economic interests in these businesses as business entities and as
sources of income are only indirectly involved in government decisions regarding the
governance structure of the proposed IRWMG.

TWSA – There is no indication from your account of the facts that the TWSA initiated
the decision regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG or is a party in, or subject of, the
proceedings within the meaning of Regulation 18704.1. Accordingly, it appears that your
economic interest in the TWSA as a source of income is only indirectly involved in the decision.
We caution, however, that this conclusion is based solely upon the facts provided. If additional
facts reveal that the TWSA or a representative of the TWSA is, or has been, identified as a
potential member of the IRWMG or as a potential candidate for a position with the IRWMG, it is
likely that the TWSA will be considered directly involved in decisions regarding the governance
structure of the IRWMG.

Personal Financial Effects – As for an official's economic interest in his or her personal
finances, an official's economic interest in his or her personal finances is deemed to be directly
involved in the governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect on the economic
interest. (Regulation 18704.5.)

Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
economic interests?

Materiality

Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to each economic interest.

Grant Management Services Businesses – For economic interests in business entities
indirectly involved in a decision, including business entities that are a source of income to an
official, the materiality standard is given at Regulation 18705.1(c).

The thresholds for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the business.
While we do not know the size of your business, Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) provides that the

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 8

financial effect of a governmental decision on a business, not publicly traded and relatively
modest in economic size, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

However, you should consult Regulation 18705.1(c) to ensure that you identify the
materiality threshold actually appropriate to your grant management services businesses.

TWSA – The materially standard for an economic interest in a source of income that is a
government entity and only indirectly involved in the decision, is provided in Regulation
18705.3(b)(2). The threshold for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the
entity. While we do not know the size of the TWSA, Regulation 18705.3(b)(2)(F) provides that
the financial effect of a governmental decision on a small governmental entity, with gross annual
receipts of $100,000 or less, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

"(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity's gross
annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.

"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional
expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $2,500 or more.

"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the
entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more."

Again, you should consult Regulation 18705.3(b)(2) to ensure that you identify the
materiality threshold actually appropriate to the TWSA.

If the TWSA is directly involved in the decision, as analyzed above, the materiality
standard is provided in Regulation 18705.3(a), which states:

"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source
of income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before
the official's agency, is deemed material."

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 9

In other words, the size of a financial effect on a directly involved source of income does
not matter. If there is any financial effect at all, even "one-penny," that effect is presumed to be
material.

In addition to the materiality standards for sources of income such as the TWSA
described above, there is a separate materiality standard that applies in cases where there is a
"nexus" between duties owed to a source of income and to the official's public agency. When a
public official is paid by another entity to accomplish some action that is within the official's
public decisionmaking authority, any financial effect on the source of income is presumed
material. The "nexus test" is set out at Regulation 18705.3(c):

"(c) Nexus. Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is
a source of income to a public official is deemed material if the public official
receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."

The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a
purpose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official, we presume that the
employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official capacity. (Yarnell
Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) Typically, a "nexus" is found in situations where the official is
also a high-level employee with direct influence and control over his or her employer's
management or policy decisions. (Moser Advice Letter, No. A-03-147; Low Advice Letter, No.
A-99-305.)

Personal Finances – An effect on an official's personal finances is material as stated in
Regulation 18705.5(a), which provides:

"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period. When
determining whether a governmental decision has a material financial effect on a
public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the
official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets
and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered."

Foreseeability

Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his or
her economic interests, the next step is to determine whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that
the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 10


be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra, at 198.)
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby,
supra, at 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination that must be
left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.

Accordingly, the Commission cannot determine what financial effect the decision may
have on your economic interests. Because the Commission is not a finder of fact, the
determination of whether the decision regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests must be left
up to your informed judgment. Barring a nexus, as discussed above, you may participate in a
governmental decision in which the TWSA and your grant management services businesses are
only indirectly involved, so long as the reasonably foreseeable financial effects on the TWSA
and businesses are less than the applicable materiality standard under Regulation 18705.3(b)(2)
and the reasonably foreseeable effect on your personal finances is less than $250 (Regulation
18705.5(a)).

However, in the event that the TWSA is or becomes directly involved in the
governmental decision regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG, your facts indicate a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the TWSA. While ultimately the determination of
whether the financial effect is material must be left up to your informed judgment, the financial
effect on your economic interest in the TWSA is presumed to be material if the TWSA is
directly involved in the decision. Thus, if the TWSA is directly involved in a decision regarding
the governance structure of the IRWMG, you may not participate in the decision unless you can
rebut the presumption of materiality by showing that the governmental decision will not have
any effect on the TWSA.

Finally, the financial effect on your economic interest in the TWSA is presumed to be
material if there is a nexus between the duties owed to the TWSA and the BCWC. Under this
"nexus" test, your facts indicate a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your economic
interest in the TWSA if a goal or purpose of your employment with the TWSA would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by a decision regarding the governance structure of the
IRWMG. However, the Commission cannot determine whether a nexus exists without a full
description of both your duties under your contract with the TWSA and your duties with the
BCWC. Thus, the decision must be based upon your informed judgment. If you determine that
a decision regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG would achieve, defeat, aid, or
hinder a goal or purpose of your employment with the TWSA, you may not participate in the
decision.

File No. A-10-098
Page No. 11

Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception to the
conflict-of-interest rules?

Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally. (Section
87103; Regulation 18707(a).)

Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take part
in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the
"legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very specific
circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section 87101;
Regulation 18708.)

However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to your circumstances, so we will not
address them further.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By:   Brian G. Lau
     Counsel, Legal Division

using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a

87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(l).) A "public official" is ;every member, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .." (Section SjO+S.) As a member of the
BCWC, you are a public official within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official-"makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her

of the proposed IRWMG.
Step Three: What are your..economic interests?',
Of the economic interests recognized under the Act2, those interests implicated by your
accoLlnt of the facts are the following:
I our analysis is limited kr the economic interests yru have identified.
Business Entity - A public official has an economic interest in a business entity3 in which
he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section
87103(a) and (d); Regulation 13703.1(a) and (b).)
Source of Income - A public official has an economic interest in any source of income,
including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within the i2 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
Personol Financial Effects - A public official has an economic interest in his or her
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the
"personal financial effects" mle. (Section 87103; Regulation 1s703.5.)
Grant Management Services - You have provided the fact that you operate a grant
management business as a sole proprietor. Presumably, you have an investment interest in this
business entity of $2,000 or more and, therefore, an economic interest in this business entity
under Section 87103(a). Regardless of Section 87103(a), as a sole proprietor, you are an
employee/manager of the business entity and have an economic intiresl in this business entity
under Section 87103(d). Moreover, if you receive income of $500 or more for your servicesln
the 12 months prior to the govemmental decision, you also have an economic interest in this
business as a source of income. (Section 87103(c).)
Additionally, you have stated that you provide grant management services as a
subcontractor for another sole proprietor. However, you have indicated that you have jointly
marketed your grant management services with those services of the other prtprietor and have
previously presented your services as a partnership with the other proprietor. t-lnder Regulation
18703'1(c), an official has an economic interest in a business entity that is "otherwise related', to
another business entity in which the official has an economic interlst pursuant to Section
87103(a) or (d). Similarly, under Regulation 18703.3, an official alsohas an economic interest
in a business entity as a sollrce of income if the business entity is "otherwise related" to another
business entity in which the official has an economic interest pursuant to Section 87103(c).
Business entities are o'otherwise related" if any one of the following tests is met:
"(A) One business entity has a controlling ownership interest in the other
business entity.
"(B) There is shared management and control between the entities. In
determining whether there is shared management and control, consideration
should be given to the following factors:
I Section 82005 provides: "Business entity" means any organization or enterprise operated fclr prgtit,
including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership. tirm. business trust. joint r.ntur., syndicate, corporation,
or association. This detlnition does not include nonprotits or governmental entities.
"(i) The same person or substantially the same person owns and manages
the two entities;
"(ii) There are common or commingled funds or assets;
"(iii) The business entities share the use of the same offices or employees,
or otherwise share activities, resoLlrces or personnel on a regular basis;
"(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working relationship between
the entities; or
"(C) A controlling owner (507o or greater interest as a shareholder or as a
general partner) in one entity also is a controlling owner in the other entity."
(Regulation 1 8703. l(dX2).)
Under the facts you have presented, it appears that your grant management services
business is otherwise related to the grant management services business of the other proprietor.
Thus, it appears that you also have economic interests in the related grant management services
business as both a business entity and a source of income.
Clients of the Grant Manaqement Services Businesses - As a sole proprietor of a grant
management services business, you may also have economic interests in the clients or customers
of this business as sources of income. Under Section 82030(a), if a public official owns a 10-
percent or greater interest in a business, customers who are sources of income to that business
are also sources of income to the public official. Provided that your income derived from any
particular client or customer aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the
governmental decision, you also have an economic interest in the client or customer as a source
of income. (Section 87103(c).)
In regards to the clients of the grant management services business for which you are
employed as a subcontractor, the Commission typically advises that a contractor is the sole
source of income to subcontractors, even though subcontractors are normally paid from funds
collected from the contractor's client. (See Sauer Advice Letter, No. 4-95-373.) Moreover, a
source of income to a business entity is not typically considered a source of income to a public
official unless the official owns a lO-percent or greater interest in the business. Howevei, we
have previously advised that a sollrce of income to a general contractor may be considered a
sollrce of income to the subcontractor if the general contractor's business is sufficiently related to
the subcontractor's business under the tests of Regulations 18703.3(a)(2) and 18703.1(d).
(Dctysog Advice Letter, No. I-06-098.) Accordingly, you also have an economic interest in a
client or customer of the otherwise related grant management services business if your share of
the income derived from the particular client or customer aggregates to $500 or more in the l2
months prior to the governmental decision.
For purposes of this analysis, the only client of either grant management services
business you have identified is the TWSA.+ Assuming that you have received income from the
TWSA of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision for your grant management
services, it appears that you have an economic interest in this client u, Jrour"" of income
notwithstanding the fact that you provide services as a subcontractor for another sole proprietor
and do not have a direct contract with the TWSA.s
California State University of Chico Research Foundation - Assuming you have received
income of $500 or more from the foundation in the 12 months prior to the govemmental
decision, you also have an economic interest in the foundation as a source of irr.o*". (Section
87102(c).) However, based upon the facts provided, there is no indication that the foundation
may be financially affected by governmental decisions regarding the governance structure of the
IRWMG' Barring additional facts, it does not appear that your economic interest in the
foundation is implicated. Thus, we do not discuss your economic interest in the foundation
further. If additional facts indicate a potential financial effect on the foundation, we recommend
seeking additional assistance providing all relevant facts.
Personal Finances - A public official always has an economic interest in his or her
personal finances. A govemmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the
decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or
her immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Step Four: Are your economic interests directty or indirectly involved in the decision?
Business Entities/Sottrces of Income - Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity
or source of income is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency when that
business entity or source of income, either directly or by an agent:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is thi subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denia"l or
a Based upon the facts provided, we are unable to analyze potential contlicts of interest you may have
based upon economic interests in clients, other than the TWSA, including but not limited to the private ..cleantech"
companies for which you currently provide services and the Town of Paradise. prior to making, participating in
making, or int'luencing any governmental decision you will need to determine whether a cont'li.lt of inte.est ,iay
arise based upon economic interests in each of your clients. If you have additional questions related to any
economic interests in your clients, other than the TWSA, it is advisable that you ."qu"rt further assistance providing
all relevant facts.
s while the Act states that "government salary" is not included in the definition of ..income" (Section
82030(bX2))' compensation fbr your services as a consultant and independent contractrlr does not constitute
government salary. (See Rainey Advice Letter, A-98- l0l .)
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in governmental
decisions are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704.t(U), tSZO5.l(a)(2), and
Regulation I 8705.3(b).)
- From the facts provided, neither your grant
management services business nor the related grant management services business initiated the
proceedings or are a party in, or subject of, the proceedings within the meaning of Regulation
18704.1. Accordingly, your economic interests in these businesses as businesJentities and as
sources of income are only indirectly involved in govemment decisions regarding the
governance structure of the proposed IRWMG.
TWSA - There is no indication from your acconnt of the facts that the TWSA initiated
the decision regarding the govemance structure of the IRWMG or is a party in, or subject of, the
proceedings within the meaning of Regulation 18704.1. Accordingly, it upp"urt that your
economic interest in the TWSA as a source of income is only intltieitly inv^olved in the decision.
We caution, however, that this conclusion is based solely upon the facis provided. If additional
facts reveal that the TWSA or a representative of the TWSA is, or has been, identified as a
potential member of the IRWMG or as a potential candidate for a position with the IRWMG, it is
likely that the TWSA will be considered directly involved in decislons regarding the governance
strLlcture of the IRWMG.
Personal Financial Effects - As for an official's economic interest in his or her personal
finances, an official's economic interest in his or her personal finances is deemed to be iirectly
involved in the govemmental decision if facts ruggesi any financial effect on the economic
interest. (Regulation 18704.5.)
Steps Five and Six: Witl there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
economic interests?
IVlateriality
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to each economic interest.
- For economic interests in business entities
indirectly involved in a decision, including business entities that are a source of income to an
official, the materiality standard is given at Regulation 1g705.1(c).
The thresholds for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the business.
While we do not know the size your businesses, Regulation 18705.1(cXa) provides that the
financial effect of a governmental decision on a business, not publicly traded and relatively
modest in economic size, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
However, you should consult Regulation 18705.1(c) to ensure that you identify the
materiality threshold actually appropriate to your grant management services businesses.
TWSA - The materially standard for an economic interest in a source of income that is a
government entity and only indirectly involved in the decision, is provided in Regulation
18705.3(b)(2). The thresholds for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the
entity. While we do not know the size of the TWSA, Regulation 18705.3(b)(2XF) provides rhat
the financial effect of a governmental decision on a small govemmental entity, with gross annual
receipts of $100,000 or less, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity's gross
annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.
"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional
expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $2,500 or more.
"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the
entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more.',
Again, you should consult Regulation 18705.3(b) (2) to ensure that you identify the
materiality threshold actually appropriate to the TWSA.
If the TWS A is directly involved in the decision, as analyzed above, the materiality
standard is provided in Regulation 18705.3(a), which states:
"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a soLlrce
of income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before
the official's agency, is deemed material.',
In other words, the size of a financial effect on a directly involved source of income does
not matter. If there is any financial effect at all, even "one-penny," that effect is presumed to be
material.
In addition to the materiality standards for sources of income such as the TWSA
described above, there is a separate materiality standard that applies in cases where there is a
"nexlls" between duties owed to a source of income and to ttre officiat's public agency. When a
public official is paid by another entity to accomplish some action that is within rhe official,s
public decisionmaking authority, any financial effect on the soLlrce of income is presumed
material. The "nexus test" is set out at Regulation 1g705.3(c):
"(c) Nexus. Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is
a source of income to a public official is deemed material if the public official
receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision.,'
The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a
purpose that may be advanced by what he or she does as apublic official, *" pr.r,r.ne that the
employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official capacity. (yctrnell
Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) Typically, a "nexus" is found in situations where the official is
also a high-level employee with direct influence and control over his or her employer,s
management or policy decisions. (Moser Advice Letter, No. 4-03-147; Low Advite Letter, No.
A-99-30s.)
Personal Finances - An effect on an official's personal finances is material as stated in
Regulation I 8705.5(a), which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $250 in any L2-month period. when
determining whether a governmental decision has a *ot"iiul financial effect on a
public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the
official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets
and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered.,'
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his or
her economic interests, the next step is to determine whether it is "reasonably foreseeable,, that
the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interesi is ..reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to
be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re
Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (1n re Thorner, supra, at l9g.)
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (ln re Oglesby,
supra, at 7 I), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination that musf be
left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.
Accordingly, the Commission cannot determine what financial effect the decision may
have on your economic interests. Because the Commission is not a finder of fact, the
determination of whether the decision regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests must be left
Llp to your informed judgment. Barring a nexLrs, as discussed above, you may participate in a
govemmental decision in which the TWSA and your grant management services businesses are
only indirectly involved, so long as the reasonable foreseeable financial effects on the TWSA
and businesses are less than the applicable materiality standard under Regularion 1g705.3(bx2)
and the reasonably foreseeable effect on your personal finances is less than $250 (Regulation
18705.5(a).
However, in the event that the TWSA is or becomes directly involved in the
governmental decision regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG, your facts indicate a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the TWSA. While ultimately the-determination of
whether the financial effect is material must be left up to your informed judgment, the financial
effect on your economic interest in the TWSA is preiumid to be materiit i{tneTWSA is
directly involved in the decision. Thus, if the TWSA is directly involved in a decision regarding
the governance structure of the IRWMG, you may not participate in the decision unless you "ori
rebut the presumption of materiality by showing that thi govemmental decision will not have
any effect on the TWSA.
Finally, the financial effect on your economic interest in the TWSA is presumed to be
matertal if there is a nexus between the duties owed to the TWSA and the BCWC. Under this
"nexus" test, your facts indicate a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your economic
interest in the TWSA if a goal or purpose of your employment with the TWSA would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by a decision rigirding the govemance structure of the
IRWMG. However, the Commission cannot determini whether a ne^us exists without a full
description of both your duties under your contract with the TWSA and your duties with the
BCWC. Thus, the decision must be based Lrpon yollr informed judgmeni. If you determine that
a decision regarding the governance stmcture of the IRWMG would achieve, defeat, aid, or
hinder a goal or pllrpose of your employment with the TWSA, you may not participate in the
decision.


Page No. I I
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception to the
confl ict-of-interest rules ?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public lenerally. (Section
87 103; Regulation 18707(a).)
Additionally' in certain rare circ-umstances, a public official may be called upon to take part
in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the
"legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very specific
circumstances where the government agency would bi paralyred from acting. lsection g7101;
Regulation 18708.)
.. However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally,, or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to your circumstan"""r, ,o we will not
address them further.

QUESTION: May you make, participate in making, or influence a government decision by the Butte
County Water Commission (the "BCWC") regarding its recommendation to the Butte County
Board of Supervisors related to the governance stmcture of a proposed Integrated Regional
Water Management Group (the "IRWMG") despite providing grant management services under
contract to the Thermalito Water and Sewer Agency (the TWSA")?
| 'Ihe Political Refirrm Ast is contained in Government Code Sections tt 1000 through 91014. All statutory
let'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Conrmission are contained in Sestions I 8l l() through 18997 of Title 2 of the Calitirrnia Code ol Regulations. All
regulakrry rcf'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the CalitbrniaCode of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
",fi;r

CONCLUSION: Because the Commission is not a finder of fact, the determination of whether a decision
regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on your economic interests must be left up to your informed judgment. you may
take part in a governmental decision regarding the governance strLlcture of the IRWMG only if
all of the following conditions are met:
o The TWSA is not, and does not become, directly involved in the decision.
There is no "nexus," as described below, between your duties owed to the TWSA and
your duties with the BCWC.
The reasonably foreseeable financial effects on your economic interests in the TWSA and
your grant management services businesses are less than the applicable materiality
standards-under Regulation 18705.3(b)(2), and the reasonably foreseeable effect on your
personal finances is less than $250 (Regulation 18705.5(a)).
No additional facts indicate a potential financial effect on any other economic interest
you may have.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 July 19, 2010 Susan Strachan Butte County Water Commission 40 Via Morro Ct Chico, CA 95929 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-098 Dear Ms. Strachan: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as Government Code Section 1090 and common law conflict of interest, or incompatible activities restrictions your agency may impose. QUESTION May you make, participate in making, or influence a government decision by the Butte County Water Commission (the "BCWC") regarding its recommendation to the Butte County Board of Supervisors related to the governance structure of a proposed Integrated Regional Water Management Group (the "IRWMG") despite providing grant management services under contract to the Thermalito Water and Sewer Agency (the "TWSA")? ________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-10-098 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION Because the Commission is not a finder of fact, the determination of whether a decision regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests must be left up to your informed judgment. You may take part in a governmental decision regarding the governance structure of the IRWMG only if all of the following conditions are met: • The TWSA is not, and does not become, directly involved in the decision. • There is no "nexus," as described below, between your duties owed to the TWSA and your duties with the BCWC. • The reasonably foreseeable financial effects on your economic interests in the TWSA and your grant management services businesses are less than the applicable materiality standards under Regulation 18705.3(b)(2), and the reasonably foreseeable effect on your personal finances is less than $250 (Regulation 18705.5(a)). • No additional facts indicate a potential financial effect on any other economic interest you may have. FACTS You are a member of the BCWC, a position appointed by the Butte County Board of Supervisors. The primary duties
